english: to wait, re žig ma bsád-par sdód-čig (re zhig ma bsad par sdod cig) wait a little yet before beginning to kill Dzl.; skád-čig kyaṅ sdód-pai loṅ méd-par (skad cig kyang sdod pa'i long med par) without waiting even for a moment Glr.; Ld.: *ltós-te dád-če* (*ltos te dad ce*) to wait and see whether etc.; *sám-te dád-če* (*sam te dad ce*) to wait for, hope for, to look forward to, *gúg-te dád-če* (*gug te dad ce*) id.; mdó-sde ˳di ˳tsó-žiṅ sdód-na (mdo sde 'di 'tso zhing sdod na) as long as the authority of this book is acknowledged Dom.; *zag daṅ kyir-kyir dad dug* (*zag dang kyir kyir dad dug*) W. (this thing) always remains round (crooked), it will not get straight. — 3. to be at home, *dẹ' yọ'* (*de' yo'*) he is at home, *dẹ' me'* (*de' me'*) he is not at home C.; to live, reside, settle at B. and col.; bka-sdód (bka' sdod) Lex., C.: 1. attendant, waiting servant, 2. aid-de camp.

1. Lex. and C. spider. — 2. summary, contents, spyi sdom (spyi sdom) 1. table of contents, index S.g. 2. general introductory remarks, introduction, also sdom-tśig (sdom tshig); sdóm-la (sdom la) summarily, to be brief, in short.

I. vb., pf. bsdams (bsdams), bsdoms (bsdoms), fut. bsdam (bsdam), bsdom (bsdom), imp. sdom(s) (sdom(s)), W. *dám-če* (*dam ce*) 1. to bind, lčags-sgróg-gis (lcags sgrog gis) to fetter Cs.; to bind or tie fast, to pinion; to bind up, to dress, wounds. — 2. to fasten, to fix firmly, e.g. by a screw-vice; kro-čùs (kro chus) by melted metal, i.e. to solder; so, to press, grind, or strike the teeth together, to gnash, as in anger Pth.; to fasten securely, the door Dzl., Pth.; rtsá-kʼa (rtsa kha) to close an opened vein Med.; hence in general, 3. to stanch, stop, to cause to cease, rtsa-krág śór-ba (rtsa khrag shor ba) the bloody flux Med.; to bind, constrain, render harmless, to neutralize, nyés-pa (nyes pa) an evil Lex., Sch. — 4 W. *káb-ša dam dug* (*kab sha dam dug*) the shoe pinches. — 5. to make morally firm, to confirm, spyód-pa (spyod pa), one's conduct, to conform it strictly to the moral law. — 6. with or without bdag-nyid (bdag nyid), to bind one's self, to engage Cs. — 7. to add together, to cast or sum up, rgyud bži bsdóms-pas leu ༡༥༤ (rgyud bzhi bsdoms pas le'u 154) all the four Gyud together have 154 chapters; yóṅs-su bsdús-pa-la (yongs su bsdus pa la) taking all together Tar. II. sbst. obligation, engagement, duty, sdóm-pa ten-pa (sdom pa ten pa) Glr., ˳dzin-pa ('dzin pa) Cs., to enter into an engagement, to bind one's self to perform a certain duty, mí-la ˳bógs-pa (mi la 'bogs pa) to bind a person by duty, by oath, to swear in Glr (e.g. in convents, in the relations of priests and laymen); sruṅ-ba (srung ba) to be true to one's duty, to keep one's engagements; ˳čor ('chor) a duty is violated Glr.; ṅá-lu sdóm-pa med (nga lu sdom pa med) I have renounced my vow Glr. — sdóm-pa gsum (sdom pa gsum), acc. to Glr. and other more recent authors, are: so-tár (so thar) (v. so-só (so so)), byaṅ-séms (byang sems), and gsaṅ-sṅágs-kyi sdom-pa (gsang sngags kyi sdom pa). Comp. sdom-ltóṅ (?) (sdom ltong) neck-bell, bell attached to the neck of cattle. — sdom-byéd (sdom byed) 1. one that binds, by duty etc. 2. an astringent medicine Cs. — sdom-gzér (sdom gzer) rivet of a pair of scissors or tongs Sch.
Sch.: a ball; a round tassel.

1. (like ὄψον) that which gives relish to food, seasoning, condiment, esp. túg-sdor (thug sdor) that which gives substance to soup, viz. meat; tsa-sdór (tsha sdor) salt and meat. — 2. spice, sdór-gyi rkyál-pa (sdor gyi rkyal pa) spice-bag S.g.; sdor-t'ál (sdor thal) spice-powder Sch. —

sign, i.e. 1. gesture, čágs-pa ˳dód-pai brda máṅ-du bstán-nas (chags pa 'dod pa'i brda mang du bstan nas) making many wanton gestures (or giving hints, intimations v. 2), lág-brda (lag brda) signs with the hand, saṅs-rgyás la gsól-čig čes lág-brda byas (sangs rgyas la gsol cig ces lag brda byas) they beckoned to him to ask Buddha Dzl.; *mig-da táṅ-če* (*mig da tang ce*) W. to give a hint with the eye, to wink. — 2. indication, intimation, symptom, token, mi-rtág ˳gyúr-bai brdao (mi rtag 'gyur ba'i brda'o) it is an indication of their frail condition Thgy.; symbol Pth., brdar (brdar) as a symbol, symbolically; de gaṅ yin ˳drí-bai brda stón-pa (de gang yin 'dri ba'i brda ston pa) to ask for a thing by symbolic signs, in symbolic language Glr.; brda spród-pa (brda sprod pa), ˳pród-pa ('prod pa), sbyór-ba (sbyor ba), ˳grol-ba ('grol ba) to explain, describe, represent, with accus., and prob. also with genit.: yin-lugs-kyi brda ˳gról-ba (yin lugs kyi brda 'grol ba) Mil. to explain the essence or nature of things (ni f.); meton. dei brda či lags (de'i brda ci lags) what may be the symbolical meaning of it Mil. — 3. word, ˳bód-pai brda ('bod pa'i brda) interjection Liś.; ˳dúl-bai brda ('dul ba'i brda) word out of the Dulwa Zam. dris-pai brda-rnyiṅ (dris pa'i brda rnying) an obsolete word for being asked, Lex.; brdá-sgyur-pa (brda sgyur pa) Sch. interpreter, dragoman Sch.; brdai blá-ma (brda'i bla ma) is
